retrieved_docs.title: Prosthetic Heart Valve -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: As the heart continuously beat throughout a lifetime, there is cyclic stress and strain on the native heart valves.  The left side of the heart, in particular, is associated with higher pressures which range between approximately 100 mmHg to 150 mmHg.  As a result, the left-sided valves (i.e., aortic and mitral) have a higher predisposition to disease compared to their right-sided counterparts. A diseased valve that can cause hemodynamic compromise or affect the quality of life will often warrant evaluation regarding possible prosthesis.

retrieved_docs.title: Bacterial Spores -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Endospores can resist inactivation from ethanol treatment. [3] They can also survive high temperatures up to 150°C, making specific Gram-positive species heat resistant. Further, bacterial spores can show typical viability signs at temperatures near absolute zero. Endospores are resistant to chemical agents, eg, triphenylmethane dyes, and can even protect the bacterial cells against ultraviolet radiation, extreme pH gradients, drought, and nutrition depletion. Endospores germinate back into vegetative cells (an active bacterial cell that undergoes metabolism) when surrounding environmental conditions favor bacterial growth and reproduction. Several stimulants, such as optimal close-to-body temperature and diffusion of nutrients and water through bacterial cell walls by altering their surface tension, revert bacterial cells to their active vegetative cells. The process of spore formation is a multistep process. It starts from bacterial DNA replication, followed by the forespore formation, which is, by definition, the pinching of the cellular plasma membrane between the replicated chromosomes. Then, a cortex forms between the inner and outer membrane by extending the second cellular membrane to enclose the forespore with calcium and dipicolinic acid. Finally, the external spore coat surrounds the endospore before its release. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Brudzinski Sign -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Brudzinski's sign is characterized by reflexive flexion of the knees and hips following passive neck flexion. To elicit this sign, the examiner places one hand on the patient's chest and the other hand behind the patient's neck. The examiner then passively flexes the neck forward and assesses whether the knees and hips flex. Upon passive neck flexion, a positive test results when the patient flexes his knees and hips. [3] During the process of performing the test, the hand on the chest prevents the patient from reflexively lifting his chest off the bed which decreases the specificity of the test. While the pathophysiology for the hip/knee flexion is not completely understood, the theory is that hip and knee flexion occurs as an involuntary reflex to compensate and help reduce meningeal irritation. Passive neck flexion causes spinal cord movement and stretching of the meninges, resulting in pain for patients with meningitis. The thinking is that the involuntary hip/knee flexion occurs to create maximal relaxation of the meninges, reducing pain. [2]

retrieved_docs.content: Genomic database studies may reveal some pathogenic variants and mutations. [16] Genetic mutations do not necessarily imply that the disease undoubtedly occurs. [1] Genetic testing and future prognostications are nowhere crystalline. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Brudzinski Sign -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Bacterial meningitis affects more than 1 million people per year globally and ranks as one of the top ten most deadly infectious diseases. [1] Bacterial meningitis has a high frequency of neurologic sequelae and mortality and is treatable with antibiotics, which makes the diagnosis extremely important and time-sensitive. As compared with other febrile illnesses, bacterial meningitis is relatively rare. Because of this, many clinical tests have been described to help providers differentiate meningitis from other causes of fever and headache. Two of the most commonly used are Kernig's and Brudzinski's signs. The original report of Brudzinski's sign was in the late 1800s by Dr. Josef Brudzinski. At that time, he described four maneuvers to aid in the clinical diagnosis of meningitis. The four maneuvers he described included the Brudzinski's reflex, the cheek sign, the nape of the neck sign and the symphyseal sign. [2] The most popular of the maneuvers is the nape of the neck sign, currently known as Brudzinski's sign.

retrieved_docs.title: Fitness for Duty and Return to Work -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Fitness for work is a dynamic concept due to the changing nature of these 2 variables. Assessments are required primarily at the beginning of a job relationship, after a health problem emerges, or periodically when transferring positions within the same employment, especially for hazardous, physically demanding, or safety-sensitive jobs.

retrieved_docs.title: Duty to Warn -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: This concept also encompasses patients visiting the emergency department. [17] In the emergency department, most threats are made under the influence of drugs, alcohol, or anger. Emergency physicians often encounter patients for the first time, so it is difficult to assess the gravity of their threats and foresee the victims. [18] Duty to warn and duty to report are integral domains of the Ethics checklist in research with ethical, legal, and social implications. [19] The Ratzan criteria for determining the suitability of sharing an unsolicited observation about bystanders include: A high probability of a serious disease A high likelihood that the individual is unaware of the condition Adequate knowledge of the disease The urgency of the diagnosis Provision of a private conversation [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Nonviral Myocarditis -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: German physician Joseph Friedrich Sobernheim coined the term "myocarditis" in 1837, which translates to "inflammation of the myocardium." However, for a long time, there was no clear consensus on what truly constituted myocarditis. In the nineteenth and early twentieth century, the term was a catch-all diagnosis for many non-valvular heart diseases, including what we now recognize as hypertensive and ischemic heart diseases. [1] Even after myocardial ischemia emerged as a separate and significant entity, the term "myocarditis” was used interchangeably with other cardiomyopathies. The confusion partly stemmed from the diversity of its clinical manifestations, which can range anywhere from nonspecific symptoms like fever, myalgias, diminished exercise tolerance to hemodynamic collapse and sudden death. It was only in 1995 that the World Health Organization and the International Society and Federation of Cardiology classified the different cardiomyopathies and defined myocarditis as "an inflammatory disease of the myocardium, diagnosed by established histological, immunological, and immunohistochemical criteria." [2] However, as the diagnosis relies on the infrequently performed endomyocardial biopsy (EMB), its actual incidence remains unknown. Myocarditis can be mild and self-limiting with minimal ventricular dysfunction, to fulminant with severe hemodynamic compromise requiring inotropic agents or mechanical circulatory support. When associated with cardiac dysfunction, the term for the condition is inflammatory cardiomyopathy. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: Biohazard Levels -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Four classifications of biosafety levels (BSLs) exist. Each level contains specific recommendations for a clinical microbiology laboratory focusing on laboratory practices, safety equipment, and facility construction. As each level progresses, it includes additional biosafety considerations from the previous level. For example, BSL-2 has kept the components of BSL-1 with further requirements, and the same applies to BSL-3 (BSL-2 with additional requirements) and BSL-4 (BSL-3 with additional requirements). The complexity of each level aligns with infectivity, disease severity, the microorganisms’ ability for transmission (including exposure routes), and the nature of the laboratory work to be performed.

retrieved_docs.title: Six Sigma Method -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: In the define phase, problems and issues within the process are outlined from both the business and client perspectives. This may include launching a team, gathering information, planning the project, and setting goals. For example, a project may define a medical error the organization wants to address. During the measure phase, further narrowing of the project focus occurs, and baseline data are collected. In the analyze phase, data are examined to help identify the root cause of an issue and help remove inefficiencies.

retrieved_docs.title: Musculoskeletal Examination -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Observation begins with accessing any visible gross abnormalities of skin and other components of the musculoskeletal system. Palpation uses from light to firm pressure to identify and quantify the abnormalities of the musculoskeletal system, pain/tenderness, trigger points. Normal or abnormal findings that could be elicited by observation and palpation include: symmetry/asymmetry - skin color and appearance, rash, ulcers, lack of sweating hair abnormalities - warmth and heat - Swelling including effusions, nodules, and inflammatory findings like synovial and periarticular thickening - muscle atrophy, tone, contractures, and spasms - crepitations - Joint deformities including spine like kyphosis and scoliosis. Manipulation consists of different techniques to access the range of motion (ROM), strength, sensations, reflexes, and gait. The proper evaluation consists mainly of testing strength (evaluate individually the muscle capacity and integrity), range of motion (evaluate the joint independently, it's restrictions, and hypo or hypermobility), reflex and sensory function (evaluate dermatomes, reflex and sensory function, to identify possibles correlations and dysfunctions between musculoskeletal and neural system), gait analysis (evaluate the integrated functions of locomotion), and trigger points (to evaluate myofascial pain, presence of trigger points and association with patient symptoms).

retrieved_docs.title: Straight Leg Raise Test (Lasegue sign) -- Definition/Introduction -- Examination Techniques
retrieved_docs.content: Recent recommendations emphasize the importance of clear communication with the patient to avoid discomfort and improve test accuracy. Proper positioning and technique are crucial to avoid false positives and ensure reliable results. In addition, maintaining patient comfort can help minimize muscle tension, which might otherwise influence the test outcomes. Noteworthily, ankle dorsiflexion during the SLR test may exaggerate the pain, although it is not part of the Lasegue sign. [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Incident Reporting -- Definition/Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Medical errors currently represent a serious public health issue, as they pose a severe threat to patient safety. The introduction of new clinical approaches, procedures, and laboratory techniques accompanied by increased bureaucracy in the life of a physician has resulted in tremendous challenges in his or her practice. Numerous studies over recent decades have shown an increased incidence of burnout syndrome and suicide rates in physicians and personnel of allied health care providers across several countries. Diagnostic errors in medicine are not infrequent, although our approach to these situations has changed notably from 40 years ago, as today there is a shift from placing blame upon an individual to identifying the cause of a medical error, as well as the application of policies to limit complications and prevent future such medical errors.

retrieved_docs.title: Periprosthetic Joint Infection -- Evaluation -- Diagnostic Criteria for Periprosthetic Joint Infection
retrieved_docs.content: The 2018 Musculoskeletal Infection Society (MSIS) definition of hip and knee PJI is evidence-based and validated criteria. [28] The criteria are a scoring-based definition consisting of major and minor criteria. The criteria should be used cautiously for adverse local tissue reaction, crystal deposition disease, or slow-growing organisms. The diagnosis of PJi can be made if one major criterion is met. (see Table 1. 2018 MSIS Major Criteria for the Diagnosis of PJI)

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1522
retrieved_docs.content: The medical taxonomy places “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions” as a distinct category that includes the specific manifestation labeled “غشي.” The term “Symptoms” is described as closely related and possibly synonymous with “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions,” indicating substantial overlap in meaning. Moreover, the broader grouping “diseases and injuries” is identified as the parent of “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions,” positioning these vague clinical signs and symptoms as subcomponents within the larger domain of diseases and injuries. This hierarchical and synonymous relationship helps clinicians organize and interpret poorly defined clinical presentations within the overall framework of disease and injury.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1973
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms are closely related to, and may be considered synonymous with, the signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, reflecting the overlap between patient‑reported experiences and observable clinical manifestations in poorly characterized disorders. In this framework, diseases and injuries are placed under the broader category of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, indicating that many inadequately defined pathologies present with nonspecific clinical findings that are captured by this umbrella term. Together, these relationships underscore the interconnected nature of clinical presentations (symptoms) and the underlying, often vague, disease or injury processes, highlighting the importance of comprehensive evaluation when managing patients with ill‑defined conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 1184

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1771
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms are closely related to and may be considered synonymous with the broader category of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions. In this hierarchical framework, diseases and injuries are classified as having the parent category of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, indicating that they are understood through the manifestation of such nonspecific signs. A specific example of a child element within this category is الانهيار في الدورة الدموية (circulatory collapse), which represents a distinct clinical manifestation that can arise from ill‑defined conditions. Together, these relationships highlight that the identification of symptoms and signs, including serious events like circulatory collapse, is essential for recognizing and managing diseases and injuries that present without clear, well‑defined diagnostic criteria.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 2675
retrieved_docs.content: Symptoms are closely related to, and may be considered synonymous with, the signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, reflecting an overlap in terminology where both terms describe the observable manifestations and patient‑reported experiences associated with poorly characterized medical disorders. This conceptual equivalence highlights the importance of recognizing that “symptoms” and “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions” often refer to the same clinical features, facilitating clearer communication in diagnosis and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 1057
retrieved_docs.content: grouped under a unified late‑effect classification that is further subdivided by anatomical region. This structured relationship aids clinicians in linking acute injury diagnoses with their potential long‑term sequelae and facilitates comprehensive patient management across different injury types and body regions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1826
retrieved_docs.content: The category “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions” is presented as a parent concept that includes the specific child entity “غشي.” In this framework, the general term “Symptoms” is described as closely related to, and potentially synonymous with, the broader phrase “signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions,” indicating that the two expressions refer to overlapping clinical manifestations. Consequently, the specific manifestation “غشي” is understood to fall under the umbrella of nonspecific or ill‑defined symptomatology, and clinicians should recognize its inclusion within this broader symptom category when evaluating and documenting patient presentations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2766
retrieved_docs.content: Respiratory distress syndrome is identified as a medical condition whose broader parent term is the Arabic phrase اِضطِراب الرِّئَة, indicating that this Arabic term encompasses the concept of respiratory distress. Additionally, respiratory distress syndrome is cross‑referenced with the Arabic designation متلازمة خلل نضج الرئة, suggesting that both Arabic terms are synonymous or closely related descriptors for the same syndrome. This linkage clarifies the terminology used in multilingual clinical contexts and aids in consistent identification and communication of the condition across language barriers.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 4 -- Community 2927
retrieved_docs.content: Essential hypertension is a form of elevated arterial pressure that is identified and interpreted through measurement of ضغط الدم (blood pressure). The concept of ضغط الدم includes the entire spectrum of arterial pressures and is directly related to ضغط الدم المنخفض (low blood pressure), illustrating the continuum from hypotension to hypertension. Recognizing how essential hypertension is defined by ضغط الدم and how ضغط الدم connects with ضغط الدم المنخفض helps clinicians assess, diagnose, and manage blood pressure disorders more effectively.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2666
retrieved_docs.content: In medical terminology, a complication can be associated with the presence of multiple open wounds that themselves are not complicated, indicating that a broader clinical complication may arise even when each individual wound does not exhibit additional problems.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1550
retrieved_docs.content: Diseases and injuries are classified as a subcategory of اضطراب بولي تناسلي (genitourinary disorder) and are also placed under the broader heading of signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, indicating that many disease and injury entities within the genitourinary system present with nonspecific clinical manifestations. The term Symptoms is described as closely related to, and possibly synonymous with, signs and symptoms of ill‑defined conditions, emphasizing that what clinicians label as symptoms often overlap with the vague or poorly defined signs that accompany various diseases and injuries. This hierarchical relationship highlights the interconnected nature of disease classification, symptom terminology, and the challenge of identifying precise clinical features in genitourinary and other poorly defined conditions, which is important for accurate diagnosis and patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 2 -- Community 506
retrieved_docs.content: Signs and symptoms of ill-defined conditions is a medical concept that groups together vague and nonspecific clinical presentations. It is closely related to, and may be considered synonymous with, the broader term Symptoms. Within this ill‑defined symptom category, the specific manifestation غشي is identified as a child concept, and malaise and fatigue is also classified as a constituent sign. Malaise and fatigue is further linked to several Arabic descriptors, being mapped to الشعور بالضعف and النوام, and additionally associated with الضعف العصبي. Hierarchically, signs and symptoms of ill-defined conditions resides within the larger domain of diseases and injuries, indicating that these nonspecific signs are part of the overall disease and injury taxonomy. Recognizing these interconnections helps clinicians understand that vague symptoms such as malaise, fatigue, and غشي belong to an ill‑defined symptom cluster, guiding appropriate assessment and management within the broader context of disease classification.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 1810
retrieved_docs.content: The concept “Above reference range” is interpreted as “الحُمَّى” and is described as an increased “isa”. In the classification of febrile disorders, the entity “fever of unknown origin” includes “الحُمَّى” as a child concept. Therapeutically, the drug salsalate may be used to treat “الحُمَّى”. Within symptom coding, the identifier “ssc” is linked to the complaint “ألم في العنق” when it occurs in the setting of “الحُمَّى”. Clinically, “الحُمَّى” is associated with the cardiac rhythm disturbance “رفرفة أذينية”. Finally, the broader “Symptoms and Complaints Component” is organized with “الحُمَّى” as its parent category, reflecting the central role of fever in the presentation of related signs and symptoms.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 5 -- Community 2961
retrieved_docs.content: The designation “Allowed qualifier” is used as an etiological label for several serious medical conditions, indicating that it denotes the underlying cause of death (التموت), lower‑extremity ulceration (قرحات الطرفين السفليين), and femoral‑neck fracture (كسر عنق الفخذ). Recognizing “Allowed qualifier” as the causal descriptor for these outcomes helps clinicians classify and communicate the origins of these adverse health events.
